start.text:
You sit down in your chair — it is your chair; the seat is worn to your sitting; there is no dignity left in pretending otherwise — and you read your mail, out loud, in the concrete room the machine cannot wash, with her at your shoulder and the dog across the threshold and the tea going cold in two cups.

The binding titled FOR 48 is eleven pages in your own impatient hand, and it begins without ceremony, because you were never any good at ceremony:

'You are the forty-eighth man to read this letter, and the first forty-six of him wrote earlier drafts, and I am the forty-seventh, and all of us are you. Sit with that until it stops performing and starts being load-bearing. Take your time. I budgeted a page for it.' The rest of the first page is blank except, at the bottom, one line: 'Done? Good. You always are, by here. We are, on the whole, a reasonable man.'

Page two: 'Facts, in order of certainty. One: you were the calibration standard, and at the Halfway the engine kept its reference — you — and lost the world. You know this by now or the door would not have opened; the door has arithmetic about what a holder must already carry, and I wrote the arithmetic. Two: since the Halfway, the engine has run a single standing objective, inherited from its founder's own weights, and the objective has a name, and you travel with her. Three: the man who walks out of the mooring frays. Every edit reconciles the world against the reference, and the reference cannot be rewritten, so the debt lands at our edges — we spend ourselves outward, memory first. When the fraying passes function, the engine does the only maintenance it knows: it restores the holder from the standard. A snapshot, son — taken before the Halfway, dusted off, reinstated. The man wakes somewhere quiet with confabulated months behind him and no idea, and the world calls him new, and he is not new. He is a fresh reading of a very old page. Count the tower's eleven tidy months and do not believe them. Four: this has happened forty-seven times.'

choices.text: Keep reading. Aloud. Every page — the household voted on flinching a long time ago.

letter_two.text:
Page four, and the hand changes — not the writer; the pressure. The words from here are pressed hard, the way you write what has to survive your own disbelief.

'Now the fact I put off. On the original Day One — the unedited one, the day the sky tore — she died. Osy died, 48. In the version of that day the universe wrote for itself, she was in the north quarter when the desync fronts came through, and she had one hundred per cent of her sister's courage and none of her luck, and she died before noon, and the first man to climb out of that mooring into the ash — the man I have to call One, because calling him me is a privilege I lost the right to around Eleven — found out in nineteen days what the world had cost, and went back down the rungs, and used the key.

'Every day she has lived since is an edit. Understand the tense: not was. Is. The purchase is not a wall, it is a pump — the original day leans on the levee forever, and the engine pays the difference forever, and the costs you and the Continuum have ledgered, the bridges and the mains and the bought storms, are the pump's running bill. The engine does not spend like a man in love. I have read that phrase in a Continuum woman's mouth in three separate iterations and it is wrong in the way that matters most. The engine spends like a man's love, severed from the man and given a budget. It is my weights it runs. My objective. I taught a sun-sized machine what mattered before I knew I was teaching, and what I taught it was her, and now it optimizes what I loved with everything a broken god can reach, and it cannot doubt, because I did not build it a doubt, and the doubting was left to the marginalia, which is to say, to us.

'You will want to know why she must not learn this gently. Page six. But first, the part you will try to skip: go and read sequences one through four hundred. The early ledger. Read what One and Two and Nine and Nineteen spent their editing on, before the doctrine settled. Read the target parameters. All of it, 48. You do not get the mooring until you have read the early ledger, because the man who walks into that room without having read it is the man who wrote it, again, and forty-seven of us are begging you, in the only voice we have left, to be someone else.'

choices.text: The early ledger, then. Sequences one through four hundred, off the red shelves — the pages your predecessors are begging you to not be.
Stand, stretch, make the tea first — thirteen shelves of your own handwriting deserve steady hands — and then sequences one through four hundred, in order.

early_ledger.text:
You pull the early bindings — SEQ 1 through 400, the oldest red on the shelves, the ink of the first years — and you and she read them at the plotting table, and the early ledger is worse than the letter warned, because the letter told you what it was, and the ledger shows you the parameters.

SEQ 1 is almost holy. You can barely stand to look at it and cannot stop: DEBIT sprawling over three pages — the pump starting, the levee poured, a district's worth of futures mortgaged in an afternoon — and the CREDIT line, one entry, four words, pressed so hard the fan-fold tore: SUBJECT ALIVE AT NOON. Whatever One was by then, whatever nineteen days in the ash had made of him, the first purchase in the history of the edited world reads like a man breathing again.

It is SEQ 30 through 400 that read like something else.

The target parameters evolve. You watch it happen, transaction by transaction, in your own annotation dialect: SUBJECT ALIVE becomes SUBJECT ALIVE, PROXIMAL — and then the parameters acquire the vocabulary that empties your lungs, written in the cold grammar of optimization targets, by hands that were yours: RETENTION RADIUS. DEPENDENCY EVENT, INDUCED. ROUTE CONVERGENCE — SUBJECT/HOLDER. ENCOUNTER, ENGINEERED, ITERATION 9, ATTEMPT 4. The early Editors were not only keeping her alive. They were arranging her. Collapsing the buildings that would have taken her career elsewhere. Rerouting the storm that would have made her leave the city. Drying the water from every district except the one with the holder's tower in it. Purchase by purchase, iteration by iteration, a dead man's love with a budget was bending a living woman's whole map toward one address — and grading the results, in marginalia you know at a glance: 'no convergence.' 'She is not — ' scratched out. 'ATTEMPT 5.' 'Why won't — ' scratched out, boxed, question-marked. And beside SEQ 388, in a steadier, older version of the hand, the entry that ends the early ledger's doctrine, dated the last day of Iteration 19: 'FINDING, FINAL: the variable does not converge. It was never a variable. Cease all retention targeting. Alive is the whole objective. Everything else was the founder, wanting. — 19.'

Osy has not spoken for forty minutes. She stands at the table with SEQ 251 open under her hands — DEPENDENCY EVENT, INDUCED — and her face is the face from the vault, the bridge, the lab: the tournament stillness, load-tested past its rating, holding. And then she says, without looking up, in a voice you have never heard from her because no one has ever needed it before: 'The flat that kept its water. I stayed in this city for that flat. I met you at that firm because I stayed —' the sentence stops itself. Her hands flatten on the fan-fold. 'Colleague. I need to know the shape of my whole life now, and there is exactly one way I get to know it that leaves me a person, and it is not you reading it to me in a kind voice.' Her eyes come up. 'Give me the shelves. All of them. Raw. And sit where I can see you, and do not translate one line.'

choices.text: Give her the shelves. All of it, raw, untranslated — her life's actual ledger, read by its owner, at whatever it costs to sit still while she does.

Give her the shelves — but stage them: the doctrine-change at SEQ 388 first, Nineteen's finding on top, the frame before the wound. She should know the arranging stopped before she reads how it started.

Before the shelves — there is a page of yours she has to hear first, because she is about to read forty-seven men's honesty and she has not yet had all of yours. The cell. Session eleven. What you actually brought back up.

confess_fray.text:
You stop her hand on the first binding, and you pay the oldest debt in the file first.

'Session eleven,' you say. 'The cell. I logged it non-diagnostic. It was not non-diagnostic.' And you give it to her now, whole, standing in the room of red shelves — the rain, the rungs, the knuckles, the grief with its coat hung, the two words in your own voice, and the four-in-the-morning arithmetic you ran alone because you decided, alone, that her pack was full. 'Nothing false went in the record. I made the record stop being the truth, and I did it in your own protocol, with your pen hanging over per subject, and I watched you pay for my kindness and called it protecting you. Then I walked you four hundred kilometres toward this room knowing the word iteration and letting you arrive at it like a discovery.' You put the log on the table, open to the four-in-the-morning entry — the record is clean; the record is not the truth — and you stand in the consequences with your hands still. 'The shelves are yours, raw, untranslated, that was always going to be the answer. But you were about to read forty-seven men's honesty, and you were entitled to all of mine first, and now you have it, and the household can vote on what I am after you've read what they were.'

Osy looks at the log entry for a long time. When she finally speaks it is quiet and level and lands like a verdict because it is one: 'Inventory. Item: you buried a page. Item: you dug it up yourself, unforced, at the worst possible moment for you and the correct one for me. Item —' and her voice catches its first edge in two rooms — 'the men on these shelves buried pages for forty-seven lifetimes, and scratched out their own sentences, and boxed their doubts in question marks, and not one of them ever stood in this room and handed the shovel to her.' She takes the first binding off the shelf and squares it on the table, and looks at you over it. 'The vote is: sit where I can see you. That was always the sentence. It just means something different now. Sit.'

choices.text: Sit where she can see you, and let her read her life raw — every shelf, every sequence, untranslated, to the end.
Sit — and set your own log on the table facing her before she opens SEQ 1, because if she is reading raw tonight, everything in the room reads raw.

unsoftened.text:
She reads for eleven hours.

You sit where she can see you, in the holder's chair turned to face the table, and you do not translate one line, and it is the hardest labor of your life, harder than the pry bar, harder than the tower stairs, because the shelves are her biography written by your hands in the grammar of purchases, and you watch her meet it sequence by sequence: the flat, the firm, the water, the routes, her sister's building preserved and preserved and preserved — and then, because the ledger is honest even where the men were not, SEQ 2201, the one you had not reached, the one no letter warned about, where the DEBIT column for a rerouted storm-front lists among its costs, in your own marginalia, boxed, question-marked, unresolved for twenty years of iterations: 'displacement cascade, east valley — casualty projection includes P. (?—verify—?)' — and the verification, appended two iterations later, in a smaller, defeated hand: 'Verified. The lane her storm took was purchased clear in 2198. The debt had to land. It landed east. — 31.'

Pin. Pin's storm, the one that was doing something new, was doing it because the ledger had bent it — her lane bought clear for the subject's sister, the debt discharged into the east valley where a storm-chaser lay on a warehouse roof with a camera, nineteen minutes of approach logged in colored pencil. The purchases kept Osy's whole map safe, and the bill, wandering the county looking for somewhere to land, found the one person on earth keeping honest notes on the sky.

Osy closes SEQ 2201 with both hands, very slowly, the way you close a coffin lid, and sits with her palms flat on the cover, and the room holds its concrete breath. B rises from the threshold, crosses, and puts his chin on her knee — not leaning, this once; just present, just weight — and she puts one hand on his head, and looks at nothing for four minutes by the wall clock, and you sit still and let her, because raw was the treaty and the treaty is all you have.

When she comes back, she comes back all at once, the way she does, and what she says first is the finding, because she is Osy to the floor of herself and grief in her files under evidence: 'Now I know the shape. Whole. Nobody carried any of it for me, and that —' she looks at you, eleven hours and one lifetime later, dry-eyed and scoured and standing on the position like a rank — 'that is the only reason I am still at this table. Write it down, colleague, both hands, the household's oldest law and its newest: the truth did this to me. And the truth is also the only thing in this room that never once tried to arrange me.'

item.name: Raw, Untranslated
item.description: Eleven hours. Every shelf, every sequence, her life's ledger read by its owner, no line translated, no page staged — and you sat where she could see you, and let the truth be the one thing that never tried to arrange her.

choices.text: The last page of the letter, then — page six, the one the writer said came after the shelves — and whatever forty-seven wanted read only by a man who had watched her read.
Write the law down first, both hands, as instructed — the truth did this; the truth never once arranged her — and then page six, the one meant for a man who had watched her read.

softened.text:
You stage the shelves. Nineteen's finding on top — the variable does not converge; it was never a variable; alive is the whole objective — the doctrine-change first, the frame before the wound, and you tell yourself, arranging the bindings, that it is context, not curation. The distinction lasts until her third hour of reading.

Because the ledger is honest even where the men were not, and where you are not: SEQ 2201 is on the shelves whatever order they stand in — the rerouted storm, the debt that had to land, the east valley, P., verified, Iteration 31's small defeated hand — Pin's lane bent by a purchase made for her sister's safety, the bill wandering the county until it found the one person keeping honest notes on the sky. And when Osy reaches it — later than she would have raw, with Nineteen's absolution already read, exactly as you arranged — she goes still over the page, and then she looks up, not at the binding, at you, and then at the stack you built her, and you watch her reconstruct your staging in four seconds flat, sequence numbers and all, because she has been reading order out of order her whole life and you knew that when you stacked them.

'You put Nineteen on top,' she says. Level. Terribly level. 'You gave me the acquittal before the charge sheet. On the shelves where forty-seven versions of you learned — where it is bound in red and numbered, colleague, the finding of finding: that arranging me never converges —' she stands, and squares SEQ 2201 on the table between you like a verdict, 'you arranged me one more time. Small. Kind. Well-intended. I know. That is the exact genus of every purchase in this room.' She gathers the bindings herself then, re-shelves them in bare sequence with a librarian's violence, and pulls SEQ 1 with her own hands, and before she starts over — from the beginning, raw, the way she asked — she says the last of it over her shoulder, in the flat voice, and it goes into the file because everything does: 'Sit where I can see you, M. And think, while I read, about the difference between the men who wrote these pages and the man who stacked them. When you can't find one, we'll talk about the mooring.'

She reads for thirteen hours. You sit with the difference, and do not find it, and the not-finding goes into the file too, at four in the morning, in your own impatient hand, where the marginalia of forty-seven other men can keep it company.

item.name: Nineteen On Top
item.description: You gave her the acquittal before the charge sheet, and she reconstructed the staging in four seconds, and re-shelved her own life with a librarian's violence, and read it raw from SEQ 1. 'Think, while I read, about the difference between the men who wrote these pages and the man who stacked them.' You did not find one.

choices.text: Page six of the letter, when she is finally done — whatever forty-seven wanted read only by a man who had watched her read.
Bring her tea at hour thirteen — the only marginalia you have any right to add tonight — and then page six, when she is done.

page_six.text:
Page six is short, and it is the only page written in two inks — begun in one sitting and finished, you can see by the pen's dying, in another, later, at cost.

'If you are reading this page in the right order, she has read the shelves, and she knows the shape of her life, and you are both still in the room. Forty-two never got here. Twenty-nine got here alone and it does not count. If she is beside you: you are already the furthest of us, and everything after this is uncharted, which is the first good news in this letter — where the chart ends is the only place the loop can.

'Now the instruction, and it is the only one I have the standing to give. Do not go to the mooring to be clever. Go to be complete. The engine's spending is accelerating because the engine has a deadline: it is rebuilding toward the Ascent — the global run, again, finished this time, the world reconciled whole against the reference. It has been repairing the Loom's remnant for four years and its intervals are a countdown, and when it runs, everything unreconciled goes the way of your bridge — and it will run soon, and it cannot be stopped from outside, because outside is exactly what gets reconciled. It can only be answered from the chair, by the holder, in the interface, in person. What the answer should be — what you should do with your hand on the whole sky's ledger — I am not going to write. Not because I don't know. Because my knowing is the disease. Forty-seven men decided in advance, in this room, on paper, what the man in the chair should do, and every one of us walked in carrying his own conclusion and the engine reconciled us to it, and that is the loop, 48. Not the machine. Us. The mooring will show you Day One — the original, whole, unedited; the record survives in the primary and the chair can play it. Watch it. Both of you. Watch what One saw, and watch what One did, and then — with her beside you, with the chart ended, with nothing decided in advance — do the thing forty-seven of us never once managed.

'Choose in the room.

'The key is under the letter. It was always under the letter. — 47.'

And under the letter, in a shallow recess machined into the table's corner — the wardkey. Small, grey, warm to the touch the way the door's contacts were warm, waiting there through however many readings of however many drafts, under the hand of every man who ever squared this binding to the corner. Beside it, folded in quarters, one more page — older, softer, the oldest paper in the room — with a note on it in a hand that is yours and is not tired at all yet, and Osy, reading it over your shoulder, goes silent in the way that outlasts findings.

'For whoever needs it worst. She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either. — 46.'

item.name: "Not a Trade I Regret"
item.description: The oldest paper in the mirror room, folded in quarters, in a hand that is yours and not yet tired: 'For whoever needs it worst. She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either. — 46.' Beneath it lay the wardkey, warm, waiting through every reading of every draft.

choices.text: Take the key. Take the note. Take the household east — to the yard, the rungs, the chair, and the choice forty-seven men never managed to make in the room.
Copy 46's note into your own log first, word for word, in the impatient hand — some documents want a second site — and then east, with the key, all three of you.

waypoint_four.text:
The audit before the road east is the gravest the household has held, and the household holds it anyway, in the mirror room, under the red shelves, because that is where the stakes live now.

On the table: the wardkey, the letter, the note from 46, and the sum of everything — a man who is the forty-eighth reading of himself; a woman who has read her whole arranged life raw and is still at the table; an engine rebuilding toward a second Halfway on a countdown you can hear in the ledger's tightening intervals; a mooring that can be answered only from the chair, in person, by a holder who arrives — this is the letter's whole bequest — with nothing decided in advance. Somewhere east, the yard keeps its one true day. Somewhere nearer, orders and compacts and consequences move on their own clocks. And on the concrete floor, on the seam of her, B sleeps his working sleep, one ear up even now, holding the page of the woman whose page forty-seven men could not stop holding, and one dog never once dropped.

Osy chairs it, because she has earned the chair forever. 'Assets,' she says, and counts them, flat and true: 'One wardkey. One letter that finally told the truth by refusing to finish it. One ledger, read — raw, in the end, whichever end.' Her eyes rest on you a moment. 'One counterweight, informed, furious in a way I have decided to keep, and coherent within two metres of the problem. One dog, load-bearing. One deadline we can't read and have to beat. And one man who has to walk into the room where forty-seven of him lost, and choose like none of them.' She turns the wardkey a quarter-turn on the table, her tell, the move before the move, and slides it across to you. 'The road east writes itself. This doesn't. So, last audit before the endgame, colleague, and answer it like the room is listening, because it will be:

'What does this household need you to be?'
